# Basic Markdown

This document covers the fundamental building blocks of Markdown: headings, paragraphs, inline formatting, lists, links, blockquotes, and horizontal rules.

## Headings

# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3
#### Heading 4
##### Heading 5
###### Heading 6

## Paragraphs and Inline Formatting

This is a plain paragraph. It contains a sentence that is long enough to demonstrate how the editor handles normal body text without any special formatting applied.

You can make text **bold**, *italic*, or ***bold and italic*** at the same time. You can also ~~strike through~~ words that are no longer relevant, and highlight `inline code` for short snippets.

Inline formatting can be combined: **bold with `code` inside**, *italic with a [link](https://example.com) inside*, and `code cannot be **nested**`.

## Links

A basic link: [Markdown Guide](https://www.markdownguide.org)

A link with a title tooltip: [CommonMark Spec](https://spec.commonmark.org "The CommonMark specification")

Autolinks are written as plain URLs: https://example.com

## Lists

### Unordered

- Apples
- Bananas
- Cherries
  - Rainier
  - Bing
  - Montmorency
- Dates

### Ordered

1. Install dependencies
2. Configure the project
3. Run the development server
4. Open the app in your browser

### Mixed nesting

- First item
  1. Numbered sub-item
  2. Another numbered sub-item
- Second item
- Third item

## Blockquotes

> The best way to predict the future is to invent it.
>
> — Alan Kay

> Blockquotes can contain **inline formatting**, `code`, and [links](https://example.com).
>
> They can also span multiple paragraphs when each paragraph is prefixed with `>`.

## Horizontal Rule

Content above the rule.

---

Content below the rule.

## Line Breaks

A hard line break is produced by ending a line with two spaces.  
This line follows immediately below the previous one without a new paragraph.

A soft line break produces a new paragraph with vertical spacing between them.

This is the second paragraph.
